text: Assistance at airports within Member State territories must enable disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to travel from a designated point of arrival to the aircraft, and from the aircraft to a designated point of departure, including the processes of embarking and disembarking.

Designated points for assistance must be established at least at main terminal entrances, check-in counter areas, train, light rail, metro and bus stations, taxi ranks, other drop-off points, and airport car parks.

Airport assistance should be organized to prevent interruptions and delays, ensuring high and equivalent standards across the Community while optimizing resources, regardless of the airport or air carrier involved.

The overall responsibility for ensuring high-quality assistance at airports rests with a central body; because airport managing bodies are central to service provision, they are assigned this responsibility.

Airport managing bodies may provide assistance to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility directly or contract the service to third parties or air carriers, subject to Community law and public procurement rules.

To ensure an equitable burden and avoid disincentives for carrying disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility, assistance is funded via a charge levied on each air carrier proportionate to the number of passengers they carry to or from the airport.

Charges levied on air carriers for assistance must be transparent and commensurate with the services provided, ensuring funds are not used for other managing body activities. This aligns with the separation of accounts provisions in Council Directive 96/67/EC.

Airports and air carriers should refer to European Civil Aviation Conference (ECAC) document 30, Part I, Section 5 and its annexes—specifically the Code of Good Conduct in Ground Handling for Persons with Reduced Mobility—when organizing assistance and training personnel.
Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 of the European Parliament and of the Council, dated 5 July 2006, establishes the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility when travelling by air.

Disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs), whether their condition is caused by disability, age, or any other factor, have the same rights as all other citizens to free movement, freedom of choice, non-discrimination, and opportunities for air travel.

Disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility must be accepted for carriage and cannot be refused transport based on their disability or lack of mobility, except for reasons justified by safety and prescribed by law. Air carriers, their agents, and tour operators should make all reasonable efforts to verify any safety-related reasons that would prevent such persons from being accommodated on a flight before accepting reservations.

Regulation (EC) No 1107/2006 does not affect other passenger rights established by Community legislation, specifically Council Directive 90/314/EEC regarding package travel and Regulation (EC) No 261/2004 regarding compensation and assistance for denied boarding, cancellation, or long delays. If a single event grants a right to reimbursement or rebooking under both this Regulation and other legislation, the passenger may exercise that right only once, at their discretion.

To ensure air travel opportunities comparable to other citizens, assistance meeting the particular needs of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility must be provided at the airport and on board aircraft through the use of necessary staff and equipment. In the interest of social inclusion, this assistance must be provided without additional charge.

To fund accessibility measures, the managing body may levy a transparent, cost-related charge on the air carrier, established after consultation with the carrier, in addition to the charges referred to in Article 8(3).

Under Article 10, air carriers must provide the assistance specified in Annex II without additional charge to disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility departing from, arriving at, or transiting through applicable airports, provided the person meets the conditions set out in Article 7(1), (2), and (4).

According to Article 11, air carriers and airport managing bodies must ensure that: (a) all personnel providing direct assistance to disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility know how to meet various disability or mobility needs; (b) all airport personnel dealing with the public receive disability-equality and disability-awareness training; and (c) all new employees receive disability-related training upon recruitment, with refresher courses provided as appropriate.

Under Article 12, passengers must be compensated in accordance with international, Community, and national law if wheelchairs, mobility equipment, or assistive devices are lost or damaged while being handled at the airport or transported on board an aircraft.
Article 13 stipulates that obligations toward disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility under this Regulation shall not be limited or waived.

Under Article 14, each Member State must designate a body or bodies to enforce this Regulation for flights departing from or arriving at airports in its territory. These bodies ensure the rights of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility are respected, including compliance with quality standards (Article 9(1)) and the satisfactory implementation of Article 8 regarding charges to avoid unfair competition.
Article 15 establishes the framework for the complaint procedure.

When organizing assistance for disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs) and training relevant personnel, airports and air carriers should refer to the European Civil Aviation Conference (ECAC) document 30, Part I, Section 5 and its annexes, specifically the Code of Good Conduct in Ground Handling for Persons with Reduced Mobility.

Airport managing bodies should consider the needs of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility when designing new airports and terminals or undertaking major refurbishments. Similarly, air carriers should account for these needs when designing new or refurbished aircraft.

Directive 95/46/EC regarding the protection of personal data must be strictly enforced to guarantee the privacy of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility. Any information requested must be used solely to fulfill assistance obligations and must not be used against passengers seeking such services.

All essential information provided to air passengers must be available in alternative formats accessible to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility. This information must be provided in at least the same languages as the information made available to other passengers.

Passengers must be compensated in accordance with international, Community, and national law if wheelchairs, mobility equipment, or other assistive devices are lost or damaged during airport handling or transport on board an aircraft.

Member States are responsible for supervising compliance with this Regulation and must designate an appropriate body to carry out enforcement tasks. This regulatory supervision does not affect the right of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to seek legal redress through national courts.

Disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility who believe this Regulation has been infringed should first notify the airport managing body or the air carrier. If the matter is not resolved, they may file a complaint with the designated enforcement body of the relevant Member State. Complaints regarding assistance provided at an airport should be addressed to the enforcement body of the Member State where the airport is located.

Airport managing bodies and air carriers should, where possible, consider the needs of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility (PRMs) when designing new airports, terminals, or aircraft, as well as during major refurbishments.

The processing of personal data for disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility must strictly adhere to Directive 95/46/EC. Information requested must be used solely to fulfill assistance obligations and must not be used against passengers seeking these services.

All essential information provided to air passengers must be available in alternative formats accessible to disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility, and must be provided in at least the same languages as the information made available to other passengers.

Passengers must be compensated for the loss or damage of wheelchairs, mobility equipment, or assistive devices occurring during airport handling or transport on board aircraft, in accordance with international, Community, and national laws.

Member States are responsible for supervising compliance with this Regulation and must designate an appropriate body for enforcement. This supervision does not affect the right of disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to seek legal redress through national courts.

Disabled persons or persons with reduced mobility who believe this Regulation has been infringed should first bring the matter to the attention of the airport managing body or the air carrier. If satisfaction is not obtained, they may file a complaint with the designated Member State enforcement body.

Complaints regarding assistance at an airport should be addressed to the enforcement body of the Member State where the airport is located. Complaints regarding assistance provided by an air carrier should be addressed to the enforcement body of the Member State that issued the carrier's operating licence.

Member States must establish and apply effective, proportionate, and dissuasive penalties for infringements of this Regulation, which may include ordering the payment of compensation to the affected person.
In the event of a conflict between the provisions of this Regulation and those of Directive 96/67/EC, this Regulation shall prevail.

The application of this Regulation to Gibraltar airport is without prejudice to the respective legal positions of the Kingdom of Spain and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland regarding the sovereignty dispute over the territory where the airport is situated.

Application of this Regulation to Gibraltar airport is suspended until the arrangements in the Joint Declaration made by the Foreign Ministers of Spain and the United Kingdom on 2 December 1987 enter into operation. The governments of Spain and the United Kingdom shall notify the Council of the date of entry into operation.

Air carriers, their agents, or tour operators are prohibited from refusing to accept a flight reservation or refusing to embark a person with a disability or reduced mobility at an applicable airport, provided the person holds a valid ticket and reservation.

An air carrier, agent, or tour operator may refuse a reservation or embarkation for a person with a disability or reduced mobility if: (a) it is necessary to meet safety requirements established by international, Community, or national law, or requirements set by the authority that issued the air operator's certificate; or (b) the size of the aircraft or its doors makes embarkation or carriage physically impossible.

If a reservation is refused based on safety requirements or physical impossibility (aircraft size), the air carrier, agent, or tour operator must make reasonable efforts to propose an acceptable alternative to the person concerned.

Persons with disabilities or reduced mobility (and any accompanying persons) who are denied embarkation on the grounds of their disability or reduced mobility are entitled to reimbursement or re-routing as provided for in Article 8 of Regulation (EC) No 261/2004, provided all safety requirements are met.

Under the same safety conditions that allow for the refusal of carriage, an air carrier, agent, or tour operator may require that a person with a disability or reduced mobility be accompanied by another person capable of providing the necessary assistance.
Directive as amended by Regulation (EC) No 1882/2003.

Airport managing bodies are responsible for providing assistance to enable disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to: communicate their arrival and request assistance at designated points, move to the check-in counter, register baggage, and proceed from check-in to the aircraft, including the completion of emigration, customs, and security procedures.

section: Airport Assistance: Aircraft Boarding and Disembarking
text: Airport managing bodies must provide assistance to enable disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to: board the aircraft and disembark from the aircraft (using lifts, wheelchairs, or other appropriate aids), move between the aircraft door and their seats, and store or retrieve baggage on the aircraft.

Airport managing bodies must provide assistance to enable disabled persons and persons with reduced mobility to: proceed from the aircraft to the baggage hall, retrieve baggage, complete immigration and customs procedures, move to a designated point, reach connecting flights in transit (including assistance on air and land sides and between terminals), and access toilet facilities.

When a disabled person or person with reduced mobility is assisted by an accompanying person, that person must be allowed, upon request, to provide the necessary assistance within the airport and during embarking and disembarking.

Ground handling of necessary mobility equipment is required. For electric wheelchairs, a 48-hour advance warning is required, and handling is subject to aircraft space limitations and relevant dangerous goods legislation.
Damaged or lost mobility equipment must be temporarily replaced, although the replacement is not required to be on a like-for-like basis.
Ground handling must be provided for recognized assistance dogs where relevant.
Information necessary for taking flights must be communicated in accessible formats.
Under Annex II, air carriers are responsible for the carriage of recognized assistance dogs in the cabin, subject to national regulations.
